duration: <15 seconds (flew by, saw it dorsally for ~5 sconds, saw it in profile for ~5 seconds).
specific_location: Greenlee Preserve. In the dense vegetation between Waneka Lake and Greenlee Reservoir.
description: The bird was seen briefly, but at very close range. First, it flew right in front of me (~10 feet away), giving its distinctive queeah! flight call as it passed. Right away, I suspected it was a Gray-cheeked. It landed in a shrub about 25 feet away from me. First I saw it dorsally, and I could see that it was a cold, neutral gray-brown from head to tail. The back and tail were concolor, with little if any reddish tinting to the tail. On landing, the bird kept its tail in line with the body. (Hermit typically does a tail-rise on landing.) This dorsal view lasted for about 5 seconds. Then the bird turned sideways (body pointed to my left, relative to angle of observation). The face was quite plain and "blank," lacking the buffy or orange highlights on the lores and spectacles and below the auricular region. I did not see the breast very well, but I was able to discern fairly heavy dark spotting against a background color that I do not recall (or did not see).Behaviors: As the bird flew past, I heard its flight call: a shrill, "urgent," slurred or disyllabic "queeah!" Immediately, I suspected it was a Gray-cheeked. This flight call is variable, but in its "typical" manifestation, it is distinctive, and probably diagnostic. I would say that this particular flight call was typical.  When the bird landed, in plain view, it simply stayed put. It didn't fidget about or raise its tail or anything like that.  Then it disappeared, not to be seen again. That's the way with Gray-cheeked Thrushes.Call: Flight call heard. This was, in fact, the initial basis of the identification. See below.Plumage: undetermined
